title: Supersymmetric approach to random band matrices
speaker: Tatyana Shcherbina
abstract: Random band matrices (RBM) are natural intermediate models to study eigenvalue statistics and quantum propagation in disordered systems, since they interpolate between mean-field type Wigner matrices and random Schrodinger operators. In particular, RBM can be used to model the Anderson metal-insulator phase transition even in 1d. In this talk we will discuss an application of the supersymmetric method (SUSY) to the analysis of the bulk local regime of some specific types of RBM.

proof_stages.label: Localization-universality background and band-matrix transition conjecture
proof_stages.summary: The speaker sets up localization length, Poisson versus GUE/GOE local statistics, defines random band matrices as an interpolation between Wigner and Anderson-type models, and states the physical crossover conjectures in dimensions one, two, and at least three.

proof_stages.label: Known bounds and the short-scale resolvent obstruction
proof_stages.summary: Existing rigorous bounds are shown to be far from the predicted one-dimensional scale, and the core obstruction is identified as the need to control Green functions at imaginary part of order 1/N rather than the 1/W scale accessible by standard random-matrix methods.

proof_stages.label: Supersymmetric setting and tractable observables
proof_stages.summary: The talk restricts to Gaussian and special-covariance band models, introduces the gauge-invariant block model, and explains why second correlations of characteristic polynomials form the accessible fermion-fermion sector of the supersymmetric approach to the full resolvent problem.

proof_stages.label: Rigorous supersymmetric results obtained so far
proof_stages.summary: The speaker states the characteristic-polynomial crossover theorem at W around sqrt(N), short-scale averaged density-of-states results, and gauge-invariant-model correlation and delocalization results obtained through supersymmetric analysis and comparison methods.

proof_stages.label: Grassmann and superdeterminant calculus for determinants
proof_stages.summary: Grassmann integration is recalled, with the determinant represented by a fermionic Gaussian integral and determinant ratios organized through supermatrices and the superdeterminant.

proof_stages.label: Hubbard-Stratonovich reduction to a matrix-chain integral
proof_stages.summary: For the second characteristic-polynomial correlation, the Gaussian matrix average creates Grassmann quartic terms, a Hubbard-Stratonovich transform introduces auxiliary Hermitian matrices, and integrating out the Grassmann variables yields a nearest-neighbor matrix-chain integral.

proof_stages.label: Transfer-matrix spectral mechanism for the crossover
proof_stages.summary: The matrix-chain integral is rewritten as a trace of a non-selfadjoint transfer operator, and the desired asymptotics are tied to controlling its leading eigenvalues, resolvent bounds, and the gap of order 1/W^2 that produces the W approximately sqrt(N) crossover.

examples.name: Wigner/GUE-type ensembles as the delocalized endpoint
examples.note: Wigner matrices and related classical ensembles are used as the fully delocalized comparison class, motivating band matrices as an interpolation and providing the GUE/sine-kernel benchmark for the stated results.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker identifies Wigner/GUE-type matrices as the wide-band delocalized limit and states their universal local statistics, but does not compute or verify anything on that ensemble itself.

passes.reason: Full Wigner/GUE-type matrices are used as the maximal-bandwidth delocalized endpoint of the band-matrix interpolation, with GUE also highly unitary-symmetric.
Full-band Wigner/GUE ensembles are the w≈N delocalized boundary of the band-matrix interpolation, not used to refute a claim.
It is used as the fully connected/maximal-bandwidth delocalized endpoint, with the GUE case carrying maximal unitary symmetry rather than serving as a refutation.

examples.name: One-dimensional Anderson model as the localized endpoint
examples.note: The random diagonal potential plus Laplacian in one dimension supplies the opposite Poisson/localized regime, and its contrast with Wigner matrices motivates random band matrices as an intermediate model.
examples.engagement_reason: The one-dimensional Anderson model is displayed/described as diagonal random potential plus Laplacian and cited as known to have localization and Poisson statistics, with no concrete analysis performed on it.

passes.reason: The one-dimensional Anderson model is cited as the opposite localized endpoint with Poisson statistics, not to refute a claim.

examples.name: Other ensembles with universal characteristic-polynomial correlations
examples.note: Wigner, sample-covariance, beta, and sparse ensembles are cited to illustrate that characteristic-polynomial correlations are a tractable supersymmetric observable with universal behavior in many non-band settings.
examples.engagement_reason: Wigner, sample covariance, beta, and sparse ensembles are cited as settings where characteristic-polynomial correlations are universal, but the talk does not work through those ensembles individually.
